title: Quiz: Quantum Superposition
description: Test your understanding of quantum superposition and its role in quantum computing

questions.question: For the state |ψ⟩ = (1/√3)|0⟩ + √(2/3)|1⟩, what is the probability of measuring |1⟩?
questions.explanation: The probability of measuring |1⟩ is |β|² where β is the amplitude of |1⟩. Here β = √(2/3), so P(1) = |√(2/3)|² = 2/3.

questions.question: What does the Hadamard gate do to the state |0⟩?
questions.explanation: H|0⟩ = (|0⟩ + |1⟩)/√2 = |+⟩. The Hadamard gate creates an equal superposition with positive amplitudes when applied to |0⟩.

questions.question: A qubit in superposition has a definite value that we simply don't know, similar to a coin under a cup.
questions.explanation: False! This is a common misconception. A qubit in superposition genuinely exists in multiple states simultaneously—it has no definite value. This is fundamentally different from classical probability where the value exists but is unknown.

questions.question: The states |+⟩ = (|0⟩ + |1⟩)/√2 and |−⟩ = (|0⟩ − |1⟩)/√2 have:

options.text: The same measurement probabilities but different relative phases
Identical physical properties in all respects

questions.explanation: Both states have P(0) = P(1) = 0.5, but their relative phases differ (+ vs -). This difference is detectable through interference, e.g., applying another Hadamard gate: H|+⟩ = |0⟩ but H|−⟩ = |1⟩.

questions.question: How many simultaneous states can 10 qubits in superposition represent?
questions.explanation: n qubits can represent 2ⁿ states simultaneously. For 10 qubits: 2¹⁰ = 1024 states. This exponential scaling is the basis of quantum parallelism.

questions.question: If you apply the Hadamard gate twice to a qubit starting in |0⟩, what state do you get?
questions.explanation: H² = I (identity). The first H creates |+⟩, and the second H returns to |0⟩ through interference. This is different from classical randomness, which wouldn't 'undo' itself.

questions.question: According to the No-Cloning Theorem, it is impossible to create an exact copy of an unknown quantum state.
questions.explanation: True! The No-Cloning Theorem is a fundamental result in quantum mechanics. Unlike classical bits which can be freely copied, unknown quantum states cannot be duplicated. This has implications for quantum cryptography and error correction.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid normalized single-qubit states? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a) |1/√2|² + |1/√2|² = 1 ✓. b) |1|² + |1|² = 2 ✗ (not normalized). c) |3/5|² + |4/5|² = 9/25 + 16/25 = 1 ✓. d) |i|² + |0|² = 1 + 0 = 1 ✓ (global phase doesn't affect normalization).
